perturbation.original_text: Each of them is responsible for the timely filing of such filings and any amendments thereto, and for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning such person contained therein; but none of them is responsible for the completeness or accuracy of the information concerning the other persons making the filing, unless such person knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of them is responsible for the accuracy of the information concerning such person contained therein; but none of them is responsible for the accuracy of the information concerning the other persons making the filing.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the responsibility for 'timely filing' and 'completeness' of filings and amendments, which contradicts SEC regulations regarding timely and complete disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 mandates that every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act must file an annual report. Removing the responsibility for 'timely filing' directly contradicts this requirement, as it opens a loophole where parties could delay filing without consequence, undermining the purpose of the Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 - Requirements of annual reports. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 - Requirements of annual reports. CFR § 240.13a-1 Requirements of annual reports. Every issuer having securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 l ) shall file an annual report on the appropriate form authorized or prescribed therefor for each fiscal year after the last full fiscal year for which financial statements were filed in its registration statement . Annual reports shall be filed within the period specified in the appropriate form. [ 62 FR 39767 , July 24, 1997] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text states responsibility for 'timely filing' and 'completeness', which is removed in the changed text. 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 requires issuers to file an annual report, implying a timely manner. The removal of 'timely filing' contradicts this legal expectation, as detailed in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states, 'Annual reports shall be filed within the period specified in the appropriate form,' solidifying the need for timely filing, which the changed text omits.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect until revoked by any party hereto in a signed writing provided to each other party hereto, and then only with respect to such revoking party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect until revoked by any party hereto.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'in a signed writing provided to each other party hereto, and then only with respect to such revoking party,' the contract now allows for implied or informal revocation, potentially violating SEC requirements for clear and documented termination of agreements related to securities filings.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1(k)(1)(iii) suggests that agreements amongst parties about how they will vote their shares needs to be disclosed in certain circumstances. Therefore, making this agreement easily terminable by one party creates an unstable and potentially opaque voting situation, when it is in the interest of shareholder protection that it be a firm agreement that needs to be properly terminated.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The original contract text requires a signed writing for revocation, ensuring a clear record. The changed text removes this requirement, potentially allowing for informal revocation. The Law Explanation suggests this creates an unstable voting situation, going against shareholder protection. While the scraped snippets don't directly address revocation requirements, they highlight the importance of clear agreements under 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 regarding beneficial ownership, implying that terminations of such agreements should also be explicit. The removal of the 'signed writing' requirement directly undermines this principle of clarity and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, and the rules and regulations thereunder (the "Exchange Act") the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing on behalf of each of them of any filing required by such party under Section 13 of the Exchange Act or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto) with respect to securities of Tallgrass Energy, LP, a Delaware limited partnership, and further agree to the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this Agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing on behalf of each of them of any filing required by such party with respect to securities of Tallgrass Energy, LP, a Delaware limited partnership, and further agree to the filing of this Agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and its associated rules significantly weakens the legal grounding of the agreement. Also the omission of 'amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto' implies only original filings are covered. This omission could violate securities regulations requiring full and accurate disclosure, including amendments and supplements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 77k establishes liability for false registration statements. By removing explicit reference to the Securities Exchange Act and limiting the scope to initial filings, the agreement becomes ambiguous regarding liability for inaccurate or incomplete amendments or supplements, which directly contradicts the Act's requirement for ongoing accurate disclosure.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits key references to the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and its associated rules. The original text explicitly agrees to joint filings as required under Section 13 of the Exchange Act, including any amendments, restatements, supplements, and/or exhibits. By removing this explicit reference, the modified text weakens the legal grounding and creates ambiguity regarding ongoing compliance. This omission contradicts the Act's requirement for accurate and ongoing disclosure. As stated in the Law Explanation, 15 U.S. Code § 77k establishes liability for false registration statements. By removing the explicit reference to the Securities Exchange Act, the agreement becomes ambiguous, and the omission of 'amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto' implies only original filings are covered. This violates securities regulations requiring full and accurate disclosure, including amendments and supplements.
